content:
Accelerated aging protocols provide quantitative benchmarks for assessing adhesion degradation at critical encapsulant interfaces. At 10,000 hours of accelerated aging, EVA adhesion in mini-modules falls to a level generally seen after more than 15 years of field exposure [REF:6]. This finding establishes a direct temporal bridge between laboratory and field conditions, indicating that standard test durations well below 10,000 hours capture only early-stage degradation behavior and may underestimate the full trajectory of adhesion loss over a module's operational lifetime [REF:6].

Interfacial degradation does not proceed uniformly across all bonded surfaces within a photovoltaic stack. The glass/EVA interface experiences more degradation than the EVA/cell interface [REF:5], and this differential degradation rate explains why the glass/EVA boundary -- despite being the initially stronger interface -- becomes the preferential site for delamination [REF:5]. This reversal of relative interfacial strength has direct implications for module reliability: failure progresses where the degradation rate is highest rather than where the initial adhesion is weakest [REF:5][REF:6].

Parallel findings from adhesive bond strength testing across different substrates reinforce the principle that substrate type governs degradation behavior. Three-way ANOVA revealed that substrate type (p=0.00) and aging method (p=0.00) had statistically significant effects on bond strength [REF:8]. By contrast, the adhesive approach -- comparing self-etch versus etch-and-rinse systems -- did not significantly affect bond strength (p=0.84) [REF:8]. This result indicates that the substrate-encapsulant interaction dominates post-aging adhesion performance, while the adhesive application strategy plays a secondary role. The convergence of this finding with the glass/EVA versus EVA/cell differential [REF:5] supports a broader conclusion: interfacial chemistry between the adherend surface and the polymer, rather than the polymer formulation alone, is the primary determinant of aging-induced adhesion loss.

Additional screening of candidate adhesive formulations under aggressive thermocycling conditions provides further evidence of material-dependent degradation. Nine candidate experimental adhesives were screened for physical properties including degree of conversion, surface microhardness, surface water contact angle, and microtensile bond strength, then subjected to thermocycling at 10,000 cycles between 5 degC and 50 degC or 1 year of biochemical aging in simulated human salivary esterase media [REF:4]. This dual-aging approach -- combining mechanical thermal stress with chemical degradation -- demonstrates that adhesive durability depends on multiple physical properties simultaneously [REF:4], and that no single parameter (e.g., microhardness or contact angle) predicts long-term bond retention in isolation.

Construction-grade adhesive systems exhibit analogous aging vulnerabilities. Peel adhesion of self-adhesive tapes on construction surfaces was tested before and after artificial aging to evaluate durability on different building envelope materials [REF:7]. While the specific substrate-adhesive pairings differ from photovoltaic encapsulants, the experimental approach of comparing pre- and post-aging peel forces across substrate types [REF:7] mirrors the methodology applied to EVA-based systems [REF:6][REF:5], reinforcing that substrate-dependent peel force degradation is a general phenomenon across polymeric adhesive applications.

| Interface / System | Aging Protocol | Key Observation | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| EVA mini-module | 10,000 h accelerated aging | Adhesion falls to >15-year field-equivalent level | [REF:6] |
| Glass/EVA vs EVA/cell | Damp heat aging | Glass/EVA degrades more; becomes preferential delamination site | [REF:5] |
| Dental adhesives (7 systems) | Three-way ANOVA (substrate x aging x approach) | Substrate (p=0.00) and aging method (p=0.00) significant; adhesive approach not significant (p=0.84) | [REF:8] |
| 9 experimental adhesives | 10,000 thermocycles (5-50 degC) + 1 yr esterase | Multi-property screening under dual aging | [REF:4] |
| Self-adhesive tapes | Artificial aging on construction surfaces | Peel adhesion durability varies by substrate | [REF:7] |

Several methodological limitations constrain the interpretation of these findings. The 10,000-hour EVA data [REF:6] derive from mini-module geometry, and scaling effects may influence adhesion values in full-size production modules. The glass/EVA versus EVA/cell differential [REF:5] is documented qualitatively rather than with peel force magnitudes, preventing direct quantitative comparison with other encapsulant systems. The dental adhesive and construction tape results [REF:4][REF:7][REF:8] originate from non-photovoltaic applications; while they confirm substrate-dependent aging as a universal principle, their direct transferability to DVS-PEI encapsulant systems remains unvalidated. Notably absent from the available evidence are peel force measurements for TPO-based or DVS-PEI encapsulants under standardized damp heat protocols, adhesion energy values (J/m2) as opposed to force-per-width metrics, and time-resolved degradation curves that would permit extraction of degradation rate constants.

Future investigations should prioritize systematic peel testing of DVS-PEI encapsulants at multiple damp heat durations to establish kinetic degradation profiles, with explicit comparison to EVA and TPO baselines under identical protocols.

**Key Findings**

- EVA adhesion in accelerated-aged mini-modules reaches a level equivalent to more than 15 years of field exposure at 10,000 hours of testing [REF:6].
- The glass/EVA interface degrades more than the EVA/cell interface and becomes the preferential delamination site despite its initially higher bond strength [REF:5].
- Substrate type and aging method are statistically significant predictors of post-aging bond strength (p=0.00 each), while adhesive approach is not (p=0.84) [REF:8].
- Multi-property screening of nine adhesives under 10,000 thermocycles and biochemical aging confirms that no single physical parameter predicts long-term durability [REF:4].
- Substrate-dependent peel adhesion degradation is observed across polymeric adhesive applications including construction tapes [REF:7], reinforcing its generality beyond photovoltaic encapsulants.
